clean_story:
Reanimated corpses. Society’s mirror.

The term zombie film comes from Haitian folklore’s zonbi—a corpse revived by magic, stripped of will. Early movies used zombies like puppets: slow, silent, obeying a master (White Zombie, 1932). Then Night of the Living Dead (1968) flipped the script. No voodoo. No control. Just hordes spreading like a virus, forcing survivors to barricade doors, turn on each other—mirroring Cold War fears of contagion and collapse.

Modern zombies move fast (28 Days Later, 2002), or decay mid-chase (Train to Busan, 2016). Rules vary: bite = infection? Or is everyone already infected (The Crazies, 1973)? The genre’s power isn’t gore; it’s the setup. Strip away laws, electricity, trust. What’s left? A stress test for humanity, played out in malls (Dawn of the Dead), farms (The Battery), or spaceships (Pandorum).

Three related subjects are viral outbreak narratives, post-apocalyptic survival tactics, and how folklore shapes horror.
